title: Trump Moves to Lift CAATSA Sanctions and Consider F-35 Sale to Turkey as S-400 Transfer Talks Advance
subhead: President Trump announced the US will lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and consider selling F-35 jets, conditioned on Turkey transferring its Russian S-400 system out of the country. Talks over the S-400's fate are underway with Russia, while Congress pushes back. Meanwhile, a Kurdish peace framework law heads to parliament, the Iraq-Syria Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline deal adds an overland energy route, and US strikes on Iran enter a seventh night.

sections.heading: Trump Announces CAATSA Sanctions Lift and F-35 Consideration for Turkey
sections.body:
President Trump said the United States will lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and is considering selling F-35 fighter jets to Ankara, Al-Monitor reported on July 17. The announcement, which came during the NATO Ankara summit earlier this month, formalizes a shift that had been building since Turkey's S-400 purchase triggered sanctions in 2020. The condition: Turkey must transfer its Russian S-400 air defense system out of the country.

Trump framed the move as part of a broader realignment, saying Turkey had been 'treated unfairly' under CAATSA and that the F-35 would strengthen NATO interoperability. Foreign Policy reported that the administration views the sanctions lift as a way to pull Turkey away from Russian defense dependence and lock in Ankara's strategic alignment with Washington amid the Iran conflict.

The original CAATSA sanctions, imposed in December 2020, blocked Turkey from purchasing F-35s and effectively froze defense-industrial cooperation. Their removal would reopen the door to one of the most consequential defense procurement deals in NATO history — Turkey's original order was for over 100 F-35s.
sections.whyItMatters: Lifting CAATSA and resuming F-35 sales would be the most significant US-Turkey defense realignment in a decade. It reverses the core penalty of the S-400 crisis and signals that Washington now values Turkey's strategic position — drone exports, Black Sea presence, Iran proximity — more than the precedent of sanctioning a NATO ally for buying Russian hardware.

sections.heading: Congress Pushes Back as Lawmakers Split on Sanctions Relief
sections.body:
The sanctions lift faces significant opposition in Congress. Jewish Insider reported that lawmakers are deeply divided: Republicans largely support Trump's push, arguing that Turkey is too important a NATO ally to sideline, while Democrats and some hawkish Republicans warn that removing sanctions without a verified S-400 destruction or transfer undermines deterrence against Russian arms sales globally.

Key concerns raised by opponents include whether Turkey can be trusted to follow through on an S-400 transfer, and whether lifting sanctions sends a signal to other US partners that purchasing Russian weapons carries no lasting consequences. The original CAATSA sanctions were designed precisely to punish such purchases, and some lawmakers argued that undoing them undercuts the law's purpose.

Supporters countered that Turkey's Bayraktar drone exports to Ukraine and its strategic position opposite Iran make the sanctions counterproductive. The Senate Foreign Relations Committee has not yet scheduled a vote on any blocking resolution, Jewish Insider reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Congress can potentially block or delay F-35 sales through appropriations and oversight mechanisms, but CAATSA sanctions relief is primarily an executive decision. The split means the path forward is real but not frictionless — Turkey will need to demonstrate credible S-400 movement before the jets arrive.

sections.heading: Turkey in Talks With Russia Over S-400 Transfer
sections.body:
Aviation Week reported that Turkey is in discussions with Russia over the fate of its S-400 system, exploring options for transferring the batteries out of Turkish territory. The talks are at an early stage, and the mechanism — whether the system goes to a third country, is returned to Russia, or placed under joint custody — has not been finalized.

Forbes reported that Turkey's air defense future is taking shape with no role for the S-400, citing defense officials who see the system's removal as a prerequisite for both F-35 access and deeper NATO air-defense integration, including potential participation in the Alliance's integrated air and missile defense architecture.

The S-400 has been stored at Murted Air Base near Ankara since 2021, never fully operational. Turkey paid roughly $2.5 billion for the system, and any transfer would need to address Russian contractual claims and technology-protection concerns on both sides.
sections.whyItMatters: The S-400 transfer is the linchpin. Without it, there is no F-35 sale and no full CAATSA relief. But the logistics are complicated — Russia has contractual and strategic interests in keeping the system in Turkey, and Turkey needs a face-saving mechanism. The fact that talks are happening at all is the signal; the outcome will determine whether the US-Turkey reset is real or rhetorical.

sections.heading: Kurdish Peace Framework Law Heads to Parliament
sections.body:
President Erdoğan announced that a Kurdish peace process framework law will soon be submitted to parliament, bianet reported on July 15. The law would establish a legal basis for negotiations with Kurdish political representatives and outline the scope of a demobilization and reintegration process, potentially including amnesty provisions for PKK fighters who lay down arms.

bianet also published a detailed analysis of what the proposed law contains, noting that it would create a parliamentary commission to oversee the process, set conditions for disarmament, and address the release of certain prisoners. The framework appears modeled in part on previous peace initiatives from 2013-2015, but with key differences: it is being drafted under a state of emergency framework that gives the executive broader authority, and it comes amid an ongoing crackdown on pro-Kurdish political structures.

The initiative follows the capture of PKK leader Abdullah Öcalan's renewed peace calls earlier this year and the Kurdish political movement's DEM Party endorsing a negotiated settlement. However, previous peace processes collapsed in 2015 amid mutual distrust, and the current political environment — with the CHP leadership crisis and Kurdish politicians facing prosecutions — makes implementation uncertain.
sections.whyItMatters: A formal framework law would be the first legislative foundation for a Kurdish peace process in a decade. But the credibility gap is wide: the same government pushing this law is also prosecuting Kurdish politicians and ousting opposition leaders. Whether this becomes a genuine opening or another tactical maneuver depends on implementation details that have not yet been published.

sections.heading: Iraq and Syria Sign Kirkuk-Baniyas Pipeline Deal in Washington
sections.body:
Iraq and Syria signed a memorandum of understanding in Washington on July 16 to rehabilitate the Kirkuk-Baniyas crude oil pipeline, Middle East Eye and Anadolu Agency reported. The pipeline, which runs from northern Iraq through Syrian territory to the Mediterranean port of Baniyas, has been offline since 1982. The deal was signed on the sidelines of broader energy cooperation discussions and received US backing, according to Kurdistan24 and the Associated Press.

Syria's SANA news agency confirmed the signing of two MoUs covering the pipeline rehabilitation and broader energy cooperation. Iraq's Oil Ministry said the project would provide an alternative export route that bypasses the Strait of Hormuz — a selling point that has gained urgency amid the ongoing US-Iran conflict and Hormuz shipping disruption.

Reuters previously reported that Turkey and Iraq have been discussing energy cooperation ahead of the expiry of their existing pipeline transit deal, which runs through 2027. The Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline remains operational, but Iraq has been seeking alternatives. The Baniyas route would give Iraq a second Mediterranean outlet, though rehabilitation would take years and faces security challenges in Syrian territory.
sections.whyItMatters: The Kirkuk-Baniyas deal is strategically significant but practically uncertain. It adds a second overland route to the Mediterranean that bypasses Hormuz — directly relevant to Turkey's energy transit role. But the pipeline runs through contested Syrian territory, and rehabilitation timelines are measured in years. For now, the deal is more signal than infrastructure: it shows the US is actively reshaping regional energy logistics around the Hormuz disruption.

sections.heading: US Strikes on Iran Enter Seventh Night as Conflict Shows No Sign of De-escalation
sections.body:
The US military completed its seventh consecutive night of strikes on Iran on July 17, CENTCOM confirmed, according to Anadolu Agency and MSN. Le Monde reported that the bombing has intensified, with strikes hitting Iranian bridges, port infrastructure, and military facilities across a widening arc. CBS News reported that the strikes are now targeting logistics and transportation networks, not just military sites — a qualitative escalation from earlier rounds.

Iran's IRGC claimed that two tankers hit mines in the Strait of Hormuz, according to MSN, reinforcing the threat to commercial shipping. The Washington Post reported that the conflict is settling into a grinding pattern: nightly US strikes, Iranian retaliation against Gulf states, and no diplomatic off-ramp in sight.

For Turkey, the seventh night of strikes means continued risk to energy supply lines, further pressure on the lira, and the strategic calculation that its geographic position between the conflict zone and European energy markets makes it both indispensable and exposed.
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict has moved from episodic escalation to sustained campaign. Each night of strikes deepens the disruption to Hormuz shipping, raises oil prices, and increases the strategic premium on Turkey's overland corridors — while also raising the risk that Iran retaliates against targets closer to Turkish interests.

sections.heading: CBRT Holds Rates Steady, Says Iran War Delayed Disinflation but Damage Is Limited
sections.body:
The Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey held its policy rate steady at 37%, Reuters reported, citing the bank's assessment that the Iran conflict has delayed disinflation but that the deterioration in inflation expectations remains limited. The CBRT told Daily Sabah that energy price pressures from the Hormuz disruption are being monitored but have not yet triggered a reassessment of the rate path.

Bloomberg reported earlier this month that the July inflation print will be key for the rate outlook, with the bank watching whether the conflict-driven energy shock feeds through to core inflation. The lira hit an all-time low of 47.17 against the dollar this week, and June inflation stood at 32.1% — still far above the bank's medium-term targets.

The hold decision reflects a balancing act: tightening further would push borrowing costs higher for an economy already under strain, while easing prematurely risks unanchoring inflation expectations at a moment when external shocks are intensifying.
sections.whyItMatters: The CBRT is signaling that the Iran conflict is a known variable, not a crisis trigger — yet. The real test comes with the July inflation data. If energy pass-through accelerates, the bank's 'limited deterioration' assessment will need revision, and rate hikes could return to the table.

sections.heading: Barrack Promotes Middle East Trade Corridor Cutting Out Israel
sections.body:
US Ambassador to Turkey Tom Barrack is promoting a Middle East trade alignment that would bypass Israel, Jewish Insider reported on July 17. Barrack, a Lebanese-American businessman appointed by Trump, has been advocating for a trade and logistics corridor connecting Gulf states through Turkey and the eastern Mediterranean — a route that would exclude Israeli ports.

The proposal fits into a broader pattern of regional economic realignment driven by the Hormuz crisis: if Gulf shipping is disrupted, overland routes through Turkey become more valuable. Barrack's specific framing — cutting out Israel — adds a political dimension that could appeal to Gulf partners but risks friction with Washington's traditional Israel-first Middle East framework.

The corridor concept is still in the advocacy stage, with no formal agreement or infrastructure commitments. But it signals how the Hormuz disruption is reshaping thinking about regional trade routes, and where Turkey fits as a logistics hub.
sections.whyItMatters: Barrack's corridor idea is speculative but directionally important. If Hormuz stays disrupted, the economics of overland routes through Turkey shift dramatically. The Israel-exclusion framing adds a political complication, but the underlying logic — Turkey as a transit hub between Gulf energy and European markets — is gaining traction regardless of who is advocating it.

bottomLine: Trump's decision to lift CAATSA sanctions and consider F-35 sales to Turkey — conditioned on removing the S-400 — is the most consequential US-Turkey defense shift in a decade. But it depends on a complex S-400 transfer negotiation with Moscow and faces Congressional resistance. Meanwhile, a Kurdish peace framework law is heading to parliament, Iraq and Syria signed a pipeline deal to bypass Hormuz, and US strikes on Iran entered their seventh night with no diplomatic off-ramp. Turkey's strategic value to the West has never been higher; the question is whether Ankara can convert that leverage into durable gains — or whether the moment passes when the next crisis erupts.
sources: Al-Monitor, Jewish Insider, Foreign Policy, Aviation Week, Forbes, bianet, Middle East Eye, Anadolu Agency, Kurdistan24, AP, SANA, Reuters, Daily Sabah, Bloomberg, Le Monde, CBS News, MSN, Washington Post, CENTCOM
